title: The On-Cost Gap: Why Recruitment and Labour Hire Margins Compress Every July 1
excerpt: Every July, award wage rises lift on-costs immediately while client bill rates lag behind by weeks or months. For recruitment and labour hire operators, that gap is where margin quietly disappears.
content:
The Fair Work Commission's annual wage decision lands on every award-covered business on the same day, but it does not land the same way. For most Australian SMEs, wages are one line among many on the P&L. For a recruitment or labour hire business, the pay rate charged out to host clients is the direct input to almost every dollar of revenue, so the same percentage increase behaves completely differently once it moves through the cost stack.

Superannuation, payroll tax, workers compensation premiums and leave loading are all calculated as a percentage of the base pay rate, so when that base rate steps up on the first full pay period of July, every on-cost calculated against it steps up with it. The total cost movement is consistently larger than the headline award percentage suggests, and it happens immediately. The revenue side of the ledger rarely moves at the same speed.

Client bill rates are governed by contracts, panel agreements and master service agreements negotiated months or years earlier, most of which specify a review date, a notice period, or a formal renegotiation process rather than an automatic pass-through. The gap between the day the cost increases and the day the bill rate catches up is where margin compresses every July, and the businesses that manage it well are the ones that treated it as a known, recurring pattern well before the wage decision landed.

Why the Same Percentage Rise Costs More Than It Looks

An award wage increase is usually reported as a single percentage figure, and it is tempting to apply that figure directly to the payroll line and move on. That understates the actual movement. Superannuation guarantee contributions, payroll tax above the relevant state threshold, workers compensation premiums, and any leave loading entitlements are all calculated on the new, higher base rate, not the old one. Each of these on-costs individually looks minor. Stacked together across a roster of placements or contractors, the combined effect on total cost of labour typically runs ahead of the headline percentage, and it does so from the very first payroll cycle in July.

The Bill Rate Lag Is the Real Margin Risk

The pay rate moves on day one. The bill rate charged to the host client moves whenever the contract allows it, which is rarely the same day. Some client agreements include an automatic award-linked adjustment clause, but many do not, and require a formal rate review request, a notice period, or a scheduled review date that may fall months away. In the interim, every hour billed at the old rate against the new cost base is a margin dollar quietly given up, and because it happens across dozens or hundreds of placements simultaneously, the aggregate impact is usually far larger than it appears on any single timesheet.

This is rarely visible in real time. Total revenue can keep growing as hours worked and headcount placed both increase, and a business can look busier and larger through July while its actual margin per hour is contracting underneath that growth. The variance typically only becomes visible once the monthly result is closed, weeks after the cost change has already been absorbed.

Margin Per Placement Is the Number to Track, Not Total Revenue

The metric that shows this early is contribution margin per placement, or per contractor category, tracked weekly rather than waiting for the monthly result. A roster where bill rates have not yet been reviewed against the new cost base will show margin per hour falling even as total billed hours climb, and that divergence is the signal to act on, not the total revenue figure sitting above it.

A Pricing Reset run against the updated cost base is the direct response: it identifies which client relationships have contractual room to move quickly, which require a formal renegotiation conversation, and which bill rates were already under-priced before this year's wage rise made the gap wider. For labour hire and recruitment operators across Queensland, NSW and Victoria, this is typically a conversation worth having in the weeks either side of July 1, not once the margin report for the month has already confirmed the damage.

faqs.q: How does the annual Fair Work wage increase affect recruitment and labour hire margins?
faqs.a: The wage rise increases the pay rate from the first full pay period of July, and every on-cost calculated against that rate, superannuation, payroll tax, workers compensation and leave loading, increases with it immediately. Client bill rates typically do not move at the same pace because they sit inside contracts with their own review timing. The result is a margin compression window that opens every July until bill rates catch up to the new cost base.

faqs.q: Why do on-costs rise faster than the headline award wage percentage?
faqs.a: Superannuation guarantee, payroll tax above the relevant threshold, workers compensation premiums and leave loading are all calculated as a percentage of the base pay rate rather than as a fixed amount. When that base rate increases, every one of those on-costs increases proportionally from the same pay cycle, so the combined movement in total labour cost typically runs ahead of the headline wage percentage alone.

faqs.q: When should a labour hire business renegotiate client bill rates after a wage rise?
faqs.a: The strongest position is to raise it before July 1, once the Fair Work Commission's decision is known and before the new pay rates are already running at a loss against old bill rates. Where a contract requires a formal review process, starting that conversation early gives the client's own approval cycle enough runway to land close to the same date the cost actually changed, rather than weeks or months behind it.

faqs.q: What is a healthy profit margin for an Australian recruitment or labour hire business?
faqs.a: Margin expectations vary by placement type and contract structure, so the more useful measure is margin per placement or per contractor category tracked consistently over time, rather than a single industry benchmark. A fractional CFO arrangement typically builds that tracking into a monthly reporting cadence so any drift is visible within the month it occurs rather than at year end.

faqs.q: How can a recruitment business protect margin during the July wage rise period?
faqs.a: The practical safeguard is reviewing bill rates against the new cost base before the wage rise lands, rather than after the first month's margin result confirms a problem. A Pricing Reset reviews every client rate against current on-costs and ranks which relationships have room to move quickly, which need a formal renegotiation, and which were already under-priced before this year's increase.

faqs.q: Does the July wage rise affect labour hire businesses differently across Australian states?
faqs.a: The Fair Work Commission's decision applies nationally, so the underlying mechanism is the same everywhere, but state-based payroll tax thresholds and workers compensation premium rates differ, which changes the exact size of the on-cost movement for businesses placing workers in Queensland, NSW and Victoria. Businesses operating across more than one state typically need the calculation run separately for each.
